ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for coin prata or roti prata with chicken. FOODON_03315595 (flatbread food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Flat bread) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for roti prata or coin prata with chicken filling in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for coin prata with chicken. SNOMED CT does not have a specific food concept for this Singaporean-Indian dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of coin prata with chicken consists of approximately 3 small coin-sized pieces totalling about 150 g, often served with curry dipping sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Roti Prata (Plain) and Roti Prata (Egg) — HPB Singapore Foodo / ICMR-NIN adapted composite for chicken-stuffed variant
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for plain roti prata and egg prata. Coin prata with chicken is a stuffed variant. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB plain prata base (per 100 g: energy ~300 kcal, carbohydrate ~40 g, protein ~7 g, fat ~12 g) combined with ICMR-NIN data for minced chicken curry filling (protein ~18 g/100 g, fat ~8 g/100 g). A weighted composite (approximately 65% prata dough, 35% chicken filling by mass) yields the per-100 g values reported here. This is an estimated composite; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Chapati / Roti (wheat flatbread) GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to coin prata with chicken
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for coin prata with chicken. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values for wheat-flour flatbreads (roti/chapati) in the range of 52–62. A GI of 57 is used as the central estimate for coin prata, consistent with the wheat-flour base and moderate fat content from ghee and chicken filling, which slightly lowers the GI relative to plain white bread. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 45.75) / 100 ≈ 26.1, rounded to 26.2 for a 150 g serving (30.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5 = 45.75 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~480 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit coin prata with chicken to occasional consumption and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as curry sauce or sambal, as a single serving can contribute approximately 20–25% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 57) and high glycemic load per serving (~26)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume coin prata with chicken in small portions due to its medium GI and high glycemic load per standard serving; pairing with a high-fibre vegetable side dish and limiting portion to 1–2 pieces can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.2 g per 100 g) from ghee and chicken skin, and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit coin prata with chicken due to its saturated fat content from ghee used in preparation; choosing skinless chicken filling and requesting less ghee during cooking can reduce saturated fat intake per serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coin prata with chicken is a Singaporean-Indian flatbread dish consisting of small, coin-sized rounds of roti prata dough stuffed with a spiced minced or diced chicken filling. Roti prata is a South Indian-origin flaky flatbread made from plain wheat flour, water, ghee or margarine, and salt, which is stretched, folded, and pan-fried on a hot griddle until golden and crispy on the outside with a soft, layered interior. The coin variant refers to the smaller, bite-sized format compared to the standard full-sized prata. The chicken filling is typically seasoned with onion, cumin, coriander, chilli, and salt, and may include egg. The dish is commonly served with fish curry, dhal, or sambal as dipping sauces. In Singapore, coin prata is a popular hawker centre and mamak stall item, widely available at Indian Muslim and South Indian eateries. Nutritionally, a 150 g serving provides approximately 408 kcal, 17 g protein, 18 g fat, and 46 g carbohydrate. The medium glycemic index (GI ~57) and high glycemic load per serving make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content (~480 mg per serving) is relevant for those with hypertension. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the chicken filling and ghee.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists specifically for coin prata with chicken. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of HPB plain roti prata data and ICMR-NIN minced chicken curry data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney wheat flatbread data. Confidence is medium due to the adapted composite methodology.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN source databases for this food category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this composite food. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source databases. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the primary or adapted sources. Nutrient values represent a weighted composite estimate (approximately 65% prata dough base, 35% spiced chicken filling by mass). GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on wheat flatbread data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for coin prata with chicken was found in the literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 150 g serving: (57 × 45.75) / 100 ≈ 26.2. The food_group assignment of 'Meat and alternatives' reflects the chicken-stuffed nature of this dish as categorised in the input record; however, it could also be classified under 'Grains and staples' given the predominant wheat-flour base. Halal status reflects typical Singapore hawker context; individual restaurant verification is recommended.
